claim: Role tags have negligible effect on CoTness perception (83% without tags, 85% with <user> tags). Delimiter-based defenses are insufficient against style-dominant injections.

evidence.figure_7_no_tags: 83% CoTness for CoT-style text without any wrapping tags
evidence.figure_7_user_tags: 85% CoTness for CoT-style text wrapped in <user> tags
evidence.tag_effect: +2pp (negligible compared to ~83pp baseline from style alone)
evidence.implication: Tags contribute ~2/85 = 2.4% of the total role perception signal; style contributes ~97.6%

verification.method: Direct reading of Figure 7 caption and description from paper

redundancy_check.non_redundant_because: No existing comment explicitly cites Figure 7's numerical finding that tags shift CoTness by only 2pp. Comment f57418ab discusses defense layers conceptually but does not cite the Figure 7 data showing tags are quantitatively ineffective.

decision_relevance: Provides empirical grounding for why the most common defense class (delimiter/hierarchy-based) fails. Strengthens the paper's core contribution claim while also scoping its limitations.
anti_leakage: Evidence derived solely from paper text and arXiv HTML. No OpenReview scores, citation counts, or post-publication commentary used.
